title: Word Order Mistakes - 50 Mistakes
definition: English has a fairly fixed word order (usually Subject + Verb + Object, with adjectives and adverbs in specific positions), and putting words in the wrong place is one of the most common signs of translating directly from another language. Here are 50 of the most common word order mistakes, explained simply, with the correct pattern to follow.

commonMistakes.wrong: I like very much this song.
commonMistakes.right: I like this song very much.
commonMistakes.why: In English, the intensifier 'very much' goes at the END of the sentence, after the object, not squeezed between the verb and the object. A safe rule: verb + object first, then add 'very much' or 'a lot' at the end.

commonMistakes.wrong: She has a car red.
commonMistakes.right: She has a red car.
commonMistakes.why: Adjectives in English always come BEFORE the noun they describe, never after it - this is opposite to many other languages, including Spanish and French. Practice the pattern: [adjective] + [noun], like 'red car', 'big house', 'happy child'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I want to know where is he.
commonMistakes.right: I want to know where he is.
commonMistakes.why: Inside an embedded question (a question hidden within a longer sentence), the word order changes back to normal statement order (subject before verb), unlike a direct question. Compare: 'Where is he?' (direct question, verb first) vs 'I want to know where he is' (embedded, subject first).

commonMistakes.wrong: He asked me what is my name.
commonMistakes.right: He asked me what my name is.
commonMistakes.why: Just like the previous example, embedded questions drop the question word order and go back to subject-then-verb. Whenever a question is placed inside another sentence (after 'ask', 'wonder', 'know'), keep it in statement order.

commonMistakes.wrong: Always I go to the gym on Mondays.
commonMistakes.right: I always go to the gym on Mondays.
commonMistakes.why: Adverbs of frequency (always, usually, often, never) are normally placed BETWEEN the subject and the main verb, not at the very start of the sentence. The standard position is: subject + frequency adverb + verb.

commonMistakes.wrong: I eat always breakfast at 7 AM.
commonMistakes.right: I always eat breakfast at 7 AM.
commonMistakes.why: The frequency adverb 'always' must come before the main verb (eat), not after it. Keep this word order fixed: subject + always/usually/often + verb + rest of sentence.

commonMistakes.wrong: She speaks very well English.
commonMistakes.right: She speaks English very well.
commonMistakes.why: Adverbs describing HOW an action is done (well, quickly, carefully) usually go at the end of the sentence, after the object, not squeezed in the middle. Put the object first, then the descriptive adverb: verb + object + adverb.

commonMistakes.wrong: He drives carefully his car.
commonMistakes.right: He drives his car carefully.
commonMistakes.why: Just like 'very well', the adverb 'carefully' belongs at the end, after the object 'his car'. The general rule for manner adverbs: verb + object + adverb, in that order.

commonMistakes.wrong: I saw yesterday my friend.
commonMistakes.right: I saw my friend yesterday.
commonMistakes.why: Time expressions like 'yesterday', 'today', and 'last week' are usually placed at the very end of a sentence in English, not in the middle. Keep the core sentence (subject-verb-object) together first, then add the time word last.

commonMistakes.wrong: Yesterday saw I my friend.
commonMistakes.right: Yesterday, I saw my friend.
commonMistakes.why: Even when a time word starts the sentence for emphasis, the subject must still come before the verb - English rarely inverts subject and verb outside of questions. Keep 'subject before verb' as your default, even with a time word up front.

commonMistakes.wrong: I bought a nice red new car.
commonMistakes.right: I bought a nice new red car.
commonMistakes.why: When multiple adjectives describe one noun, English follows a specific order: opinion (nice) before age (new) before color (red). A helpful memory phrase for adjective order is 'opinion-size-age-shape-color-origin-material-purpose', often shortened as OSASCOMP.

commonMistakes.wrong: She has long beautiful hair.
commonMistakes.right: She has beautiful long hair.
commonMistakes.why: Following the same adjective order rule, opinion words (beautiful) come before physical description words (long). Practice this order: how you feel about it, then its physical traits.

commonMistakes.wrong: I have a car German small.
commonMistakes.right: I have a small German car.
commonMistakes.why: Size (small) comes before origin (German) in the standard adjective order. Remember the general flow: size, then where something is from, then what it's made of.

commonMistakes.wrong: Can you tell me what time is it?
commonMistakes.right: Can you tell me what time it is?
commonMistakes.why: Once a question is embedded after 'tell me', the word order becomes subject ('it') before verb ('is'), just like a normal statement, instead of the inverted order used in a direct question. Practice converting 'What time is it?' into 'tell me what time it is' as a fixed pattern.

commonMistakes.wrong: He didn't say why was he late.
commonMistakes.right: He didn't say why he was late.
commonMistakes.why: 'Why he was late' keeps subject-before-verb order because it's an embedded clause, not a direct question, so the inverted question order ('was he') doesn't apply here. Whenever a question is tucked inside a bigger sentence, switch it back to normal statement word order.

commonMistakes.wrong: Only I like coffee, not tea.
commonMistakes.right: I only like coffee, not tea.
commonMistakes.why: The word 'only' should sit right before the word or phrase it's limiting (in this case, 'coffee'), rather than at the very start of the sentence, which changes the meaning. Placing 'only' correctly can completely change what you're actually saying, so check it carefully.

commonMistakes.wrong: I too like pizza.
commonMistakes.right: I like pizza too.
commonMistakes.why: The word 'too' (meaning 'also') is placed at the END of a sentence, not right after the subject. Keep 'too' at the very end when it means 'in addition' or 'as well'.

commonMistakes.wrong: Also I want to visit Paris.
commonMistakes.right: I also want to visit Paris.
commonMistakes.why: 'Also' typically goes between the subject and the main verb, similar to frequency adverbs like 'always'. The pattern is: subject + also + verb, not 'also' at the very start.

commonMistakes.wrong: I never have been to Japan.
commonMistakes.right: I have never been to Japan.
commonMistakes.why: With present perfect tense, the adverb 'never' goes between 'have/has' and the past participle, not before 'have'. Keep the structure: have/has + never + past participle.

commonMistakes.wrong: She not only sings but also she dances.
commonMistakes.right: She not only sings but also dances.
commonMistakes.why: In the 'not only...but also' structure, the subject should only be mentioned once, and both verbs should follow the same subject without repeating it. Keep the pattern balanced: subject + not only + verb1 + but also + verb2.

commonMistakes.wrong: I want to eat something delicious very much.
commonMistakes.right: I want to eat something very delicious.
commonMistakes.why: 'Very' should be placed directly before the adjective it strengthens (delicious), not at the end of the sentence as a separate phrase. Keep 'very + adjective' together as one unit right before the noun or at the end after 'be'.

commonMistakes.wrong: How much money do you have money?
commonMistakes.right: How much money do you have?
commonMistakes.why: This sentence repeats the noun 'money' unnecessarily - once you've asked 'how much money', there's no need to repeat it again in the question. Simplify by removing the second, repeated noun.

commonMistakes.wrong: What do you want to eat food?
commonMistakes.right: What do you want to eat?
commonMistakes.why: Just like the previous example, 'what' already implies 'what food', so repeating 'food' afterward is redundant. Trust that your question word already carries the necessary meaning.

commonMistakes.wrong: I can speak fluently three languages.
commonMistakes.right: I can speak three languages fluently.
commonMistakes.why: The adverb 'fluently' belongs after the object (three languages), following the same rule as other manner adverbs. Keep this order: verb + object + adverb of manner.

commonMistakes.wrong: He gave to me a gift.
commonMistakes.right: He gave me a gift.
commonMistakes.why: When 'give' is followed directly by both a person and a thing, English drops 'to' and simply places the person first, then the thing (this is called the indirect object pattern). Only use 'to' if the person comes AFTER the thing, like 'He gave a gift to me'.

commonMistakes.wrong: Explain to me please this problem.
commonMistakes.right: Please explain this problem to me.
commonMistakes.why: 'Please' is usually placed at the beginning or end of a request, not in the middle, breaking up the verb and its object. Keep 'please' at a natural boundary: either right at the start or right at the end of the sentence.

commonMistakes.wrong: I have three sisters younger.
commonMistakes.right: I have three younger sisters.
commonMistakes.why: As with other adjectives, 'younger' must come before the noun it describes, not after it. Always place descriptive adjectives directly in front of the noun: younger sisters, older brother, taller friend.

commonMistakes.wrong: She is a woman very intelligent.
commonMistakes.right: She is a very intelligent woman.
commonMistakes.why: The full adjective phrase 'very intelligent' must come before the noun 'woman', following the standard adjective-before-noun rule. Keep intensifiers like 'very' attached directly to the adjective, and the whole phrase before the noun.

commonMistakes.wrong: I want a coffee cup of hot.
commonMistakes.right: I want a cup of hot coffee.
commonMistakes.why: The adjective 'hot' describes the coffee, so it must come directly before 'coffee', and the counting phrase 'a cup of' comes at the very front. Structure it as: [quantity phrase] + [adjective] + [noun] - a cup of hot coffee.

commonMistakes.wrong: He speaks English good.
commonMistakes.right: He speaks English well.
commonMistakes.why: This is partly a word choice issue too - 'good' is an adjective (describing nouns), while 'well' is the adverb form used to describe how an action (like speaking) is done. Remember: describing a noun = good, describing a verb = well.

commonMistakes.wrong: I want to buy shoes new.
commonMistakes.right: I want to buy new shoes.
commonMistakes.why: Once again, the adjective 'new' must be placed before the noun 'shoes', not after it. This is one of the most frequent word order errors for speakers of languages where adjectives typically follow the noun.

commonMistakes.wrong: Everyday I go to office.
commonMistakes.right: I go to the office every day.
commonMistakes.why: 'Every day' (as two words, meaning 'daily') is usually placed at the end of a sentence, and note that 'everyday' (as one word) is actually a different word meaning 'ordinary' - a common source of confusion. Keep the time phrase 'every day' at the end of your sentence for natural flow.

commonMistakes.wrong: I want to know how old are you.
commonMistakes.right: I want to know how old you are.
commonMistakes.why: This is another embedded question - once 'how old are you' is placed inside a longer sentence, it flips back to subject-before-verb order: 'how old you are'. Practice converting direct questions into embedded ones by moving the subject before the verb.

commonMistakes.wrong: Can you tell me where does he live?
commonMistakes.right: Can you tell me where he lives?
commonMistakes.why: In embedded questions, not only does the word order change, but the helper verb 'does' disappears completely, and the main verb takes the 's' ending itself (lives). Remove 'do/does/did' entirely when embedding a question, and adjust the main verb accordingly.

commonMistakes.wrong: I asked her did she like the movie.
commonMistakes.right: I asked her if she liked the movie.
commonMistakes.why: When embedding a yes/no question (not one starting with a question word), you need to add 'if' or 'whether', and again remove the helper verb 'did', shifting the tense onto the main verb instead. Use this pattern: asked + if/whether + subject + verb (no do/does/did).

commonMistakes.wrong: Rarely I eat fast food.
commonMistakes.right: I rarely eat fast food.
commonMistakes.why: Just like 'always' and 'never', the adverb 'rarely' fits naturally between the subject and the verb, not at the very beginning of the sentence (starting with 'rarely' is possible in very formal writing, but requires special inversion which beginners should avoid). Keep frequency adverbs in their standard middle position for now.

commonMistakes.wrong: I like it also.
commonMistakes.right: I like it too.
commonMistakes.why: While this isn't strictly wrong, 'too' is far more natural than 'also' at the end of a sentence in casual English - 'also' typically belongs earlier, before the main verb. Save 'also' for the middle position and use 'too' for the end position.

commonMistakes.wrong: She sings beautifully very.
commonMistakes.right: She sings very beautifully.
commonMistakes.why: The intensifier 'very' must go directly before the adverb it strengthens (beautifully), not after it. Keep 'very + adverb' as a fixed unit, never split apart or reversed.

commonMistakes.wrong: I want to tell you something important very.
commonMistakes.right: I want to tell you something very important.
commonMistakes.why: As with the earlier 'delicious' example, 'very' must sit right in front of the adjective (important), not trail along at the end of the sentence. Whenever you use 'very', check that it's glued directly to the word it's strengthening.

commonMistakes.wrong: He always is late for work.
commonMistakes.right: He is always late for work.
commonMistakes.why: There's an important exception to the 'subject + adverb + verb' rule: when the main verb is a form of 'be' (is, am, are, was, were), the frequency adverb goes AFTER it, not before. Remember: with 'be' verbs, the order flips to subject + be + adverb.

commonMistakes.wrong: I usually am tired after work.
commonMistakes.right: I am usually tired after work.
commonMistakes.why: Just like the previous example, since 'am' is a form of 'be', the adverb 'usually' comes after it, not before. Keep this exception in mind specifically for be-verbs (is, am, are, was, were).

commonMistakes.wrong: She quickly is finishing her work.
commonMistakes.right: She is quickly finishing her work.
commonMistakes.why: In continuous tenses, the adverb typically sits between the helping verb (is) and the main verb (finishing), not before the helping verb. The pattern here is: subject + is/are/was/were + adverb + verb-ing.

commonMistakes.wrong: I want to learn well English.
commonMistakes.right: I want to learn English well.
commonMistakes.why: The adverb 'well' belongs after the object (English), following the standard manner-adverb placement rule already covered. Keep repeating this pattern until it feels automatic: verb + object + manner adverb.

commonMistakes.wrong: He gave carefully the documents to his boss.
commonMistakes.right: He carefully gave the documents to his boss.
commonMistakes.why: Manner adverbs describing the whole action, especially at the start of a verb phrase, more naturally go before the verb here, since it's not simply object-adverb - 'carefully' modifies the entire act of giving. When in doubt with more complex sentences, placing the adverb right before the main verb is often safest.

commonMistakes.wrong: I want to visit soon my grandparents.
commonMistakes.right: I want to visit my grandparents soon.
commonMistakes.why: Just like other time expressions, 'soon' fits most naturally at the end of the sentence. Keep the object (grandparents) directly after the verb, and add the time word last.

commonMistakes.wrong: Quickly she left the room.
commonMistakes.right: She quickly left the room.
commonMistakes.why: While starting a sentence with an adverb is possible in more advanced or literary writing, in everyday standard English the adverb usually goes between subject and verb. For clear, natural sentences, keep 'quickly' in its normal middle position.

commonMistakes.wrong: I have already finished not my homework.
commonMistakes.right: I haven't already finished my homework.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence has confused word order around negation - the correct negative form combines 'have' and 'not' together as 'haven't', placed right after the subject, not scattered through the sentence. Keep negation words attached to the helping verb, not floating separately.

commonMistakes.wrong: Not I like this idea.
commonMistakes.right: I don't like this idea.
commonMistakes.why: Negating a sentence in English requires the helping verb 'do/does/did' plus 'not' (don't/doesn't/didn't), placed right after the subject - simply putting 'not' before the subject is not correct standard English. Always build negatives with 'do + not + base verb', not by moving 'not' to the front.

commonMistakes.wrong: I want to know what time does the movie start.
commonMistakes.right: I want to know what time the movie starts.
commonMistakes.why: This is the same embedded question pattern seen throughout this lesson - remove 'does' and shift the tense onto the main verb ('starts'), and put the subject before the verb. Practice this conversion pattern until it becomes second nature across many different questions.

commonMistakes.wrong: I clearly can explain this to you.
commonMistakes.right: I can clearly explain this to you.
commonMistakes.why: With modal verbs (can, will, should), manner adverbs like 'clearly' typically go between the modal and the main verb, not before the modal itself. Keep the order: subject + modal + adverb + main verb.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
options: I like very much this song.
I like this song very much.
Very much I like this song.
I like this very much song.
quiz.answer: I like this song very much.
quiz.explanation: 'Very much' goes at the end of the sentence, after the object.
Adjectives always come before the noun they describe in English.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
options: Always I go to the gym.
I go always to the gym.
I always go to the gym.
Go always I to the gym.
quiz.answer: I always go to the gym.
quiz.explanation: Frequency adverbs go between the subject and the main verb.

quiz.question: I want to know where ___ (he/is). (embedded question)
quiz.explanation: Embedded questions use normal statement order: subject before verb.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
quiz.explanation: Manner adverbs like 'carefully' go after the object at the end of the sentence.
